question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any assessment that has been made of the impact the war in Iran is having on the price and supply of fertilizer.
answer_text: The war in Iran has caused shipping difficulties in the Strait of Hormuz where a third of the world’s supply of fertiliser moves through.  The latest data is also showing fertiliser price increases for farmers in Northern Ireland of around 25% since the war commenced and 45% since the start of the year. There have been reports of supply issues although fertiliser remains available at present.  It is not possible at this stage to determine the longer term impacts but I will continue to monitor the market situation.  My Officials have also been discussing the potential impacts with UK Government officials and industry representatives.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether the Education Authority's technical assessment of accommodation at St Paul's High School, Bessbrook, took into account lands adjacent to the school site which his Department has been seeking to acquire; and (ii) any action he will take to ensure pupils with special educational needs at the school are accommodated in conditions that are fit for purpose.
answer_text: The assessment of the St Paul’s High School site was based solely on the school’s existing site boundary and did not take account of the plot of land being considered for additional car parking as this has not yet been acquired. The acquisition cannot proceed without planning approval, particularly as the land lies outside development limits and carries significant associated risks.  Although pre-application discussions have taken place, no formal planning application has yet been submitted.  My officials will work with the Education Authority (EA) and should the additional land be secured, the EA will assess the feasibility of a minor works project to provide the required accommodation in parallel with the car parking proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the introduction of legislation to permanently end petroleum exploration and licensing.
answer_text: Following Executive approval of the draft Bill on 26 February, I have written to the Speaker and I will introduce it to the Assembly on 13 April.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update in relation to the review into the Child Maintenance Service.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the consultation on the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
answer_text: I published my proposals for a draft Budget 2026-27 to 2029/30 on 6 January. There is no doubt the ongoing under investment in public services by the British Government means that both the Resource and Capital DEL funding position remain extremely constrained. Our public services are facing increased demand and increased pressures with increased costs to deliver the services people rely on.In this context, my draft Budget proposals do not provide level of funding I would wish to see directed at our public services, instead they reflect what is possible within the funding available. It has not been possible to provide any Executive department with the funding it has requested. But as I have said many times, I am in listening mode. I want to hear the voices of families, workers, businesses and communities to ensure that the decisions the Executive makes in agreeing a Budget reflect the needs and aspirations of the people we serve. The consultation which will close on 3 March and the responses will be collated and provided to the Executive in advance of any Budget decisions. I therefore encourage all to participate and have their say.It is important that we grasp this opportunity, to provide departments with certainty and the ability to make long term plans, and to enable strategic investment that delivers lasting benefits for our economy, our environment and our society.In parallel to the consultation running I have been engaging with my Executive colleagues in a series of bilateral meetings.I stand ready to continue to work together with my Executive colleagues to support workers, families, communities and businesses by delivering a multi-year budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the December 2025 Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime report Totally Invisible: The experiences of domestic violence and abuse victims/survivors and children engaging with private law family court processes.
answer_text: The findings presented in this report are clear that reform is needed to ensure that responses to disclosures of Domestic Violence and Abuse in Article 8 Private Law applications are met with visible and tangible compassion from professionals and the judiciary, are grounded in informed assessment, robust analysis of information and an understanding of trauma and its impact.  I met with the Commissioner Designate in relation to this research in October 2025 and spoke at the launch event. I have subsequently nominated a number of representatives from my department to take part in a Family Court Pilot Task and Finish Working Group to examine what practical steps could be taken and the barriers that may need to be overcome to make sure the family courts consistently protect victims of domestic abuse and their children from further traumatisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will participate in the proposed scoping group on developing a new model of family law, as requested by the Victim of Crime Commissioner Designate.
answer_text: I wrote to the Victim of Crime Commissioner Designate on 13 January 2026 agreeing that the Department will join the Family Court Pilot Task and Finish Working Group. The following areas will be represented:The Family & Children’s Policy Directorate which has policy responsibility for child welfare and domestic and sexual abuse;The Community Care Directorate of the Strategic Planning & Performance Group which has responsibilities for strategic planning, commissioning services and the oversight of operational performance of the Health and Social Care Trusts’ delivery against their statutory functions; and The Office of Social Services which has responsibility for the professional standards of social work practice.I have also suggested that the Department of Finance is involved as they hold responsibility for the legislation.I am awaiting a response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 39000/22-27, to detail the timescale for the initialisation and completion of the work of the proposed scoping group, on developing a new family law model.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the actions she is taking to improve road safety.

To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the report, Totally Invisible: The experiences of domestic violence and abuse victims/survivors and children engaging with private law family court processes in Northern Ireland, Commissioner for Victims of Crime Office.
answer_text: I welcome the report of the research on family courts by Queen’s University Belfast for the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime. It is a comprehensive and thought-provoking report.One of the recommendations in the report is consideration of alternative family law models that adopt a more trauma-informed approach and place children’s voices at the centre of the decision-making process. To take this recommendation forward, the Commissioner Designate is seeking the formation of a cross-departmental (given that the Departments of Health and Finance, as well as in a more limited capacity my Department, have responsibilities in relation to family justice), multi-agency working group, to scope the feasibility of piloting a new model, and has recently written to me about this. I am supportive in principle of working collaboratively to consider how the current system could be improved to ensure the best outcomes for children and families, particularly those who have experienced domestic abuse. I have, therefore, indicated that I am happy for my Department to participate in a scoping exercise.In regard to the other recommendations in the report, I will be giving careful consideration to those that come within the responsibilities of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any provisions of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 that have (i) not been implemented; and (ii) been partially implemented.
answer_text: As previously advised in response to AQO 2660/22-27, the majority of the provisions within the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 have now been commenced. Work is, however, continuing to progress on a few remaining provisions.Section 27 – Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders My officials, in partnership with the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), continue to develop a model for Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders (DAPN/Os), tailored to the Northern Ireland context. This model will take into consideration operational capacity, costs, and, most importantly, the needs of victims.Fact-finding visits to pilot areas in England have been key to informing the design process. These visits have provided valuable insight into the practical challenges and learning from implementation elsewhere.Given the current uncertainty around likely volumes, it is considered essential to undertake a Northern Ireland pilot before wider roll-out. This will allow for a more accurate assessment of the number of qualifying cases and the associated cost and resource implications. The aim is to agree a preferred pilot model with delivery partners by the end of this financial year, with a view to introducing the pilot during the second half of 2026/27.Section 32 places a requirement on the Department to publish a statement setting out information about the level of participation by relevant personnel and staff in domestic abuse training. The Department has already published three training statements, in line with this requirement.Regarding sections 36 and 38, which provide for the prohibition of cross-examination in person in family and civil proceedings respectively, my Department’s focus continues to be on implementation in family proceedings, as this will have the greatest impact for victims. The drafting of the secondary legislation required in support of this provision is under way. Subject to emerging priorities and securing the necessary funding for the court-appointed legal representatives, the aim is to commence the provision this year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the strategic impact of programmes delivered under the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Irish Language Strategy.
answer_text: All Executive Departments are currently engaged in the development of the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy and the Irish Language Strategy through the work programme of the Cross-Departmental Working Group (CDWG). To date, the CDWG continues to build on the work of each strategy’s expert advisory panel and co-design group to ensure that each strategy is coherent, costed and accompanied by a series of achievable actions within departmental remits.The detailed timing for this work is to a large degree outside the control of my Department. There is a wide range of material to be considered and CDWG members need to take into account their existing departmental priorities, budgets and the practicalities involved in delivering on the vision.Once the CDWG has completed its work programme, and upon receipt of relevant departmental approvals, officials will bring forward both draft strategies for my consideration. I will then bring them to the Executive for consideration

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 37068/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 37068/22-27 was answered on 13 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will publish the review of speed limits.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. I launched a public consultation on speed limits on 14 January that will close on 22 April 2026. The consultation seeks views on the wider use of permanent 20mph speed limits, the lowering of national speed limit on rural single and dual carriageways, and alignment of HGV speed limits with other regions.  I would encourage as many people as possible to contribute to this significant and important piece of work to help inform the actions we take in relation to speed related matters in the future.The consultation considers evidence from across the UK, Europe and the South to identify potential opportunities for road safety improvements we can consider implementing in alignment with the Road Safety Strategy 2030.I intend to publish the findings of the consultation later in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the banking roundtable in December 2025.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the expected timeline for the reopening of Slieve Gullion South Cairn, including (i) the works required; and (ii) the funding (a) allocated; or (b) identified, to complete them.
answer_text: Specifications and costings are being developed to replace elements of the contemporary roof covering. The works will be subject to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the 26 recommendations of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing System in Northern Ireland including the Surrender Principle, to outline any action he is considering to modernise liquor licensing.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her initiative for a one-hour grace period for anyone parking in on-street spaces within Controlled Parking Zones in Newry city centre.
answer_text: To support high street shopping and hospitality over the festive season, I recently announced a pilot initiative for Newry and Lisburn, introducing an hour’s grace period for people parking on-street in parking spaces within Controlled Parking Zones (CPZs).   The initiative began on Saturday 29 November 2025 and will run until 10 January 2026, providing people up to one hour to return to their vehicles following the expiry of their paid parking event without the risk of receiving a Penalty Charge Notice.I have been listening to traders, and I understand the need to encourage people to spend more time in our town centres without worrying about parking deadlines.  Providing an hour’s grace period for parking sessions will give shoppers and traders that boost, offering greater flexibility for visitors while balancing the need to maintain an effective flow of traffic into these city centres during the festive season.  This pilot initiative will help guide my future intentions and long-term plans, for on-street parking in controlled parking zones, ensuring they meet the needs of local communities and businesses.I encourage all visitors to Newry and Lisburn to park responsibly to ensure we have safer streets, better access for disabled people, support for local business and a transport system that works for everyone.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work her Department is undertaking in relation to Community Wealth Building.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the implementation of the recommendations contained in A Fair Start - Final Report and Action Plan.
answer_text: I am currently considering the Fair Start Progress Report up to 30 June 2025. I will then be sharing this report with my Executive colleagues and once they have noted it, I will be publishing it on my departmental website.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Strategic Framework to End Violence against Women and Girls.
answer_text: We have made significant progress since launching the Strategic Framework to end violence against women and girls, just over a year ago. From the start, we said that our main focus is prevention, challenging the harmful attitudes and behaviours which can lead to violence.That is why we allocated funding of £3.2 million to the EVAWG Change Fund and are delighted to have been able to secure an additional £2.2 million which will further advance work in this area. We are seeing really positive activity taking place across a wide range of community settings, including youth groups, sports organisations, schools, businesses and the arts sector. This activity has been delivered across all 11 Councils through the Local Change Fund.	In addition, eight community and voluntary sector expert organisations are delivering activity that will strengthen the impact and reach of programmes which support schools, communities and workplaces. The Power to Change Campaign, jointly launched by The Executive Office, Department of Justice and the Police Service of NI in January 2025, targets harmful attitudes and behaviours of men and boys, and uses the active bystander approach. Working in partnership with the Education Authority, the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls youth panels have designed a board game called ‘Raise Your Game’ to support getting the Power to Change campaign messages to a wide range of youth settings.               Our new pilot campaign on coercive control addresses the issue of ‘how it makes you feel’ enabling women and girls to identify what coercive control is, and if this is something impacting them. A toolkit provides training that will help frontline service providers in knowing how to support women and girls in this situation. Work is progressing across a number of other key areas as set out in the first Delivery Plan.This is a positive start, but there is much more work to be done. Ending violence against women and girls will require everyone to play their part.  We are determined to do everything we can to create a society where women and girls are free from all forms of gender-based violence, abuse and harm.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has considered the findings of the British study Acting on Methane: Opportunities for the UK Cattle and Sheep Sectors.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider (i) introducing speed calming measures at the junction of Fords Cross, Silverbridge; and (ii) visiting Fords Cross.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resource, traffic calming schemes are taken forward through the prioritisation of those roads deemed most in need for intervention in line with the Department’s policy.My Department has previously put a number of additional safety measures on the Newry Road approach to the junction. These measures included advance Give Way signs with yellow backing boards, a SLOW marking and a Give Way sign at the junction which were in addition to large advance direction signage showing the layout of the junction. I have asked my officials to review the existing signs and road markings to determine if further safety improvements are appropriate.Concerns regarding driver behaviour should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether speed calming measures could be put in place on the Concession Road, Culloville, especially since there is a crèche using the road.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resource, traffic calming schemes are taken forward through the prioritisation of those roads deemed most in need for intervention in line with the Department’s policy.I am advised that the Concession Road, through Culloville, has an existing 30mph speed limit in place and that Clonalig Primary School, on the road, operates a part time 20mph speed limit.  A review of the collision history also indicates that the speed limit in operation is considered appropriate.Concerns regarding driver behaviour should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will replicate the Pay Related Social Insurance scheme in the south for those suffering from medical hair loss.
answer_text: The Pay Related Social Insurance (PRSI) scheme in the Republic of Ireland is administered through the Department of Social Protection and provides a range of benefits, including illness benefits, to qualifying individuals who have made sufficient contributions.  For those with medical hair loss it can provide a financial contribution towards the cost of a wig or hairpiece.  Matters pertaining to social security benefits in Northern Ireland are the responsibility of the Department for Communities.In Northern Ireland, support for individuals with medical hair loss is provided by Health and Social Care Trusts.  Patients who are eligible for wigs or hairpieces following clinical assessment, typically via dermatology or oncology pathways, will receive these free of charge as part of their care.  There is no provision in legislation that would enable a Trust to provide a patient with a voucher or cash in lieu of providing a service or as a contribution towards a private service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will present the Gender Equality Strategy to the Executive.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the progress of the Clean Air Strategy.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made on the development of the Clean Air Strategy. My officials are engaging with the other departments responsible for minimising polluting emissions to air, to agree the final actions to include within the Strategy.  It is my intention to take forward a public consultation of the Clean Air Strategy for consultation as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Culloville Road, Crossmaglen, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the actions her Department is taking to promote road safety.
answer_text: My Department has recently launched an advertising campaign on poster sites and on the back of buses which addresses the issue of the safety of school children around buses.We have also added to our Roadwise Up advertising campaign series – including pedestrian safety, drink driving and driver attitudes to risk taking.Two advertising campaigns have aired on TV this year. Our Careless driving campaign, ‘Driver Or Passenger’ which has the premise if you’re not paying attention when driving, you might as well be a passenger, and ‘Classroom’ our anti-speeding campaign. I have continued to increase subscriber numbers to Share the Road to Zero, my Department’s road safety community engagement programme to reach wider audiences in new ways. Recent supporters of this initiative include Armagh ladies captain Clodagh McCambridge, Olympic athlete Ciara McGeehan,  Liverpool’s Conor Bradley, NI Women’s football team, Ulster GAA, NIFL, Belfast Giants and Ulster Rugby.We need to encourage a societal conversation about road safety – it’s something we should all be thinking about as road users. Too many people are losing their lives on our roads. So far this year, 18 people have died. That is so much more than just a number – behind it are lives which have been shattered by the worst possible news. A loved one, a friend, a neighbour or colleague set out on what seemed like a routine journey but they didn’t reach their destination and will never return home.  We continue to work with our blue lights partners and others to highlight the stark reality of the devastation that lies behind serious and fatal road traffic collisions.This has included sharing the stories of the lived experience of emergency responders, and the heartbreak of victims’ families. I thank them for their courage in allowing us to share their stories.I would appeal to everyone in this Chamber to support and share road safety messaging to help reach as many people as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on livestock movements between GB and the north of Ireland in relation to bluetongue, given the development and roll-out of vaccinations.
answer_text: The Bluetongue (BTV) outbreak in Great Britain (GB) has made it impossible to meet the conditions on the required Export Health Certificate to move BTV susceptible animals from GB to Northern Ireland. This remains the case irrespective of the development and roll-out of BTV vaccination in GB.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on work to improve road safety on the Culloville Road, between Crossmaglen town and Culloville village.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment in safety improvements, due to limited budget resources, schemes must be assessed and prioritised to those locations where the greatest benefits can be achieved.Details of the safety improvement schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any mechanisms that his Department will put in place to ensure a just transition to net zero.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 places requirements on all Northern Ireland departments relating to delivering just transition objectives. My Department is leading on developing Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan covering the period 2023-2027. The Climate Action Plan will set out how the policies and proposals within it take account of the just transition principle and it is up to each department to ensure that they give due regard to this requirement.The Act places a duty on my Department to establish a Just Transition Commission.  A consultation on this closed in January 2025 and my officials have been carefully considering the responses received. I will be seeking agreement from my Executive colleagues to the draft Regulations that will establish the Commission, following which they will then be laid in the Assembly for debate and approval. I am also bringing forward a Green Growth Strategy that sets out our long-term vision and framework for Northern Ireland’s transition from a high to a net zero emissions society. The Strategy contains ten Executive Commitments, one of which is A Fair Transition which will ensure that the transition to Net Zero is a “Just Transition,” fair for all our citizens, supported and overseen by a Just Transition Commission, as required under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.In addition, I have recently announced a £12.3m financial package for the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture which will help to provide the necessary support to the agriculture sector to reduce its emissions. Ensuring a just and fair transition is key to ensuring our path to net zero works for everyone in society, and in recognition of this I have allocated resources in my Department to take forward broader just transition policy development in order to support the delivery of the important just transition requirements of our Climate Change Act.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Planning Improvement Programme.
answer_text: My Department has been working collectively over the last 2 years with local government and other stakeholders to deliver the Planning Improvement Programme with the aim of enhancing and making a tangible difference to the planning system in the North.Significant progress has been achieved so far.  For example, legislation has been delivered to enable councils to produce local validation checklists which will improve the quality of applications submitted which will in turn have benefits to performance. Ongoing work with statutory consultees, through the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum, has resulted in performance improvements, with the most recent statistics showing that 79% of statutory consultations were responded to within the statutory target.I want to build upon the momentum created through the initial phase of planning improvements. The next phase of planning improvement will refocus on specific areas of action and initiatives, which best support the long-term sustainability of the system, as well as improving overall performance. My officials recently completed a series of face-to-face meetings with each council and its members to discuss the findings of a ‘deep dive’ analysis of planning data in respect of the processing of planning applications. Officials have found this exercise extremely helpful. Not only has it helped to  understand a number of challenges in current operational practice and procedures which are impacting the performance of the system, but it has also informed opportunities for support, shared learning and best practice across all planning authorities, with the ultimate aim of improving performance on the ground.On 2 April 2025 a Planning Round Table event titled ‘Plan to Improve: Pathway to Progress’ was held with key stakeholders and users of the planning system. The purpose of this wider engagement was to facilitate and encourage open, honest and collaborative dialogue between the wide range of key stakeholders in the planning system. The event discussed many of the key current themes particularly relating to the performance and resilience of the planning system for the future.   The feedback from those who attended has been markedly positive. My officials are now considering the diverse perspectives, discussions, views, ideas and key messages from the event to help inform and prioritise the actions within the next phase of the improvement agenda.The importance of working collectively to make improvements cannot be underestimated and by sharing insight and expertise, both within the planning profession and across various sectors, we can all shape the path forward to deliver the best planning service possible for the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent on repairing rural roads in South Armagh from the Rural Recovery Fund, in comparison to other areas, in the last ten years.
answer_text: For a number of years now, my Department has allocated budget to a Roads Recovery Fund, which is primarily targeted towards the lower tier network. These improvements target short lengths of mainly rural roads in particularly poor condition.The table below provides detail of the spend on rural roads in each division since 2016, noting that in the 2017-2018 financial year, there was no specific allocation to the Roads Recovery Fund.My Department operates on a divisional basis, and for the purposes of this answer, the Southern Division figures will include the South Armagh area.YearNorthern£mSouthern£mEastern£mWestern£mTotal£m2016-172.03.50.54.010.02018-193.44.72.44.515.02019-202.33.11.63.010.02020-212.63.91.14.412.02021-223.65.51.56.417.02022-233.43.52.00.99.82023-242.13.30.93.710.0The annual level of funding is determined by budget availability and capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the importance and effectiveness of the Scoil Spreagtha scheme at a community level for the growth of the Irish language as part of his Department’s strategic responsibility for languages.
answer_text: My department provided around £3.6m in funding to Foras na Gaeilge in 2024 to allow them to meet their statutory responsibilities. I have no plans to reduce this funding in 2025. Decisions around funding priorities, including the level of funding provided for the Scoil Spreagtha scheme, are an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge and its Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the role that language schemes such as Scoil Spreagtha have in combatting educational disadvantage that primary school pupils can face due to the lack of opportunities to learn a second language.
answer_text: My Department has not been involved in the Scoil Spreagtha project nor has it provided any funding.  Therefore, no assessment has been carried out.In Northern Ireland, the teaching of a second language is encouraged at both Foundation and Key Stages 1 and 2. This can be either during core teaching hours or as part of their extracurricular provision. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations & Assessment has recently updated materials for modern languages designed to support language initiatives at primary.My Department does provide a range of funding to schools in order to combat educational disadvantage, including funding that can be used to create opportunities to learn a second language.Targeting Social Need (TSN) funding is allocated as part of core school budgets in recognition of the additional challenges and costs involved in supporting children and young people from disadvantaged backgrounds as well as those at risk of underachievement. TSN resources can be used in a variety of ways by schools in accordance with their own identified priorities, enabling them to be responsive to the needs of pupils, parents, families and the wider community. The Extended Schools Programme (ESP) allows those schools serving the most disadvantaged areas to offer a wide range of services or activities outside of the normal school day to help meet the learning and development needs of pupils, their families and local communities. In 2022/23, 84 language clubs were delivered in schools in receipt of Extended Schools funding. Additionally, the ESP includes a funding uplift for schools who cluster with other schools in order to share resources and work jointly on specific activities. An Irish language clustering strand was introduced in 2012/13 in order to enhance the use of the Irish language in schools and communities. In 2023/24, £260k of funding was allocated to 30 Irish-medium Clusters in the ESP. The benefits of this include enhanced language skills, cultural awareness, improved confidence of learners and increased community engagement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any immediate actions he will take to improve GP services in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: I fully recognise the pressures facing GP services in Newry, Armagh and across Northern Ireland and my Department is taking actions to facilitate the improved sustainability of primary care. A key focus is the expansion of the Multi-Disciplinary Team (MDT) model, which will be completed in the Newry district and commenced in the Armagh district as part of the first phase of the Department’s MDT programme from 2025/26 to 2028/29. This funding will ensure that patients benefit from integrated access to a wider range of healthcare professionals, including physiotherapists, mental health practitioners, and social workers, improving service accessibility and outcomes.  In instances where practices have handed back contracts and a new contractor does not come forward to take on the contract, my Department has ensured that arrangements, including both with GP Federations and Trust-managed services, are in place to ensure that patients can continue to access GP services within their areas. This includes financial and recruitment support as seen in Mullaghbawn Surgery and Kilkeel Medical Practice, where the Southern Trust is currently managing these practices. My Department is also in the process of advertising the GMS Contract for Rathkeeland Surgery, Crossmaglen, with the preferred outcome being the appointment of a new GP contractor through a formal recruitment process. To strengthen the workforce, a two year GP fellowship programme launched in September 2024 in the Southern locality, offering newly qualified GPs structured roles combining practice sessions with specialist skills development. I remain committed to ensuring that communities across Newry, Armagh and Northern Ireland can continue to access quality, sustainable GP services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he will take to ensure people in Crossmaglen and South Armagh can access GP services.
answer_text: On 26 February 2025, Rathkeeland House Surgery, Crossmaglen notified my Department that they were withdrawing from their contract to deliver general medical services.  Following completion of the notice period, their contract will end on 31 August 2025.   My Department will begin the process to secure alternative arrangements for these services.  The preferred option is to secure a GP contractor or grouping of GPs to take over the practice through a formal recruitment process.  Rathkeeland House Surgery will continue to deliver GP services and patients do not need to take any action.  They should continue to contact their practice as normal. My Department has commenced the process of informing the patients of the practice.  To ensure stability for all GP practices in the area, the Department has temporarily suspended the ability of the patients of Rathkeeland House Surgery to change GP practices.  This is only a temporary move until a new contractor is appointed and services are stabilised.  Despite the pressures on General Practice and the number of practices that have handed back their contracts in South Armagh, with the support of GP Federations and Trusts in taking on contracts, the Department has been able to ensure that patients can continue to access GP services in their areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that he visited Rathkeelan House Surgery, Crossmaglen, in June 2024, whether he was aware of the situation that the practice was in.
answer_text: I visited the Rathkeelan House Practice on 27 June 2024, there was no indication of the practice considering handing back their contract discussed at this meeting.Rathkeeland House Surgery, Crossmaglen notified my Department that they were withdrawing from their contract to deliver general medical services on 26 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any planned uplifts of grants for unpaid carers.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the number of reported dog attacks on sheep in Newry and Armagh in the last five years; (ii) for his assessment of existing legislation on livestock worrying; and (iii) to detail any further measures he plans to introduce to prevent such incidents.
answer_text: Livestock worrying is a criminal offence and those found guilty can face a fine of up to £1,000 and a court can determine that any dog involved is destroyed.  My Department is not responsible for investigating incidences of livestock worrying, including attacks on sheep, as this is the statutory responsibility of councils under The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 (the Order).Information on livestock worrying incidents is collated from all 11 councils and published on my Department’s website.  Under the Order, livestock worrying is classified as attacking, chasing or killing farmed animals, including cattle, pigs, poultry and equidae, as well as sheep. Therefore, my Department neither holds the information you are seeking nor is dog attack data recorded in the format you have requested.  You may wish to contact the relevant councils directly to determine if they can supply this information in this form.I continue to highlight the need for dog owners to comply with the law on livestock worrying, including the need to have control over their dog at all times, especially in the countryside, and my Department published guidance on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department’s work to create more good jobs.
answer_text: Question 14 AQO 1598/22-27 grouped with Question 15 AQO 1599/22-27Since my predecessor Conor Murphy set out a new economic vision for the North, my Department and its delivery partners have taken forward a wide range of initiatives to help create more good jobs.The Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill public consultation focused on four key aspects of a Good Job – terms of employment; pay and benefits; voice and representation; and work-life balance. Detailed analysis of responses is almost complete. This analysis, along with other policy considerations, will inform my decisions about the way ahead. I plan to bring Bill proposals to the Executive for agreement in the months ahead. A Good Work Charter consultation will be launched in the coming months. I look forward to working alongside the LRA, business and trade union leaders in co-designing a Charter that will support businesses here to maintain and create good jobs for all.We want to stimulate high-value-added growth by promoting industries and sectors with the potential to create high-quality jobs with good pay, conditions, security and opportunities. Last year we published action plans for seven high-potential industrial sectors, a Sub-Regional Economic Plan, and a Social Enterprise Action Plan.Continued investment in education and skills is another crucial component of the good jobs agenda. Despite the continuing budget constraints we face, we prioritised up to £12m this year for a Skills Fund that offers reskilling and upskilling opportunities for both those in and seeking employment.We also provided extra funding to grow student numbers in our universities and have taken a range of measures to widen access and participation for people regardless of their background, including a 20% increase in the maximum maintenance loan available for higher education students. Thanks to the Magee Taskforce we now have a clear action plan to expand university provision in the North-West.
